content:
Before GELU, deep networks relied heavily on **sigmoid** and **tanh**, which saturate and slow learning, and on **ReLU**, which fixed saturation but introduced a hard zero for negative inputs and a nondifferentiable point at zero.

**Hendrycks and Gimpel (2016)** proposed GELU by linking activation to the **Gaussian cumulative distribution function**, offering a smooth gate that generalizes ReLU-like behavior while allowing small negative responses.

The idea gained practical adoption when **Transformer** architectures scaled: **BERT (2018)** and **GPT-2** used GELU in feed-forward sublayers, cementing it as the default smooth non-linearity for modern language models.

Today GELU sits alongside ReLU and newer activations (e.g. Swish) as a standard choice wherever pretrained Transformer checkpoints and Hugging Face configs expect `gelu` in MLP blocks.

timeline.label: ReLU era
timeline.description: ReLU replaces sigmoid/tanh in deep CNNs with simple, fast piecewise-linear activations.

timeline.label: GELU proposed (2016)
timeline.description: Hendrycks and Gimpel define x·Φ(x) and motivate probabilistic gating as a smooth alternative.

timeline.label: Transformer adoption
timeline.description: BERT and GPT-2 FFN blocks standardize GELU, spreading it through NLP and later vision Transformers.
